segments.text:
Complement
A group of about 30 serum and cell membrane proteins that interact with one another and with other molecules of innate and adaptive immunity to carry out key effector functions. Helps eliminate pathogenic microorganisms by directly causing their lysis or by promoting their phagocytosis.

Complement
-discovered in 1894 by Bordet
-Paul Ehrlich coined the term, defining it as “the activity of blood serum that completes the action of antibody”
-Originally identified by their ability to amplify and complement the action of antibodies

Blood Clotting Pathway
https://youtu.be/R8JMfbYW2p4
Summary of Complement activation

Activation of Complement by 3 pathways
Classical-initiated by antibody binding
Alternative-initiated by foreign cell surfaces
Lectin-initiated by binding of mannose binding lectin (MBL) to surface of microorganisms.
Antibodies bound to antigen on membranes can bind C1qr2s2
22 molecules of C1 holoprotein

Pentameric IgM
Planar form             Staple form
MBP- Associated Serine Protease

Lectins
Proteins that recognize and bind specific carbohydrate targets

http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9IyufZf6QOM
Dr. Najeeb
Membrane Attack Complex (MAC)
C3 is a large, molecular weight protein (185kd) that interacts with at least 25 different soluble and membrane bound proteins
Activation of C3 to produce C3b
Complement functions (non-MAC)
Opsonization (to make tasty)

Important Terms and Concepts
Complement
Initiated by classical (antibody), alternative, lectin pathways
C1 complex
Mannose Binding Lectin
Membrane attack complex (MAC)
C3, C3b (opsonization)
Examples of non-MAC complement effects

Humoral immune response
Host defenses that are mediated by antibody present in the plasma, lymph, and tissue fluids.  It protects against extracellular bacteria and foreign macromolecules.  Transfer of antibodies confers this type of immunity on the recipient.

Cell- mediated immune response
Host defenses that are mediated by antigen-specific T cells and various nonspecific cells of the immune system.  It protects against intracellular bacteria, viruses, and cancer and is responsible for graft rejection. Transfer of primed T cells confers this type of immunity on the recipient.
http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9IyufZf6QOM

Notes on designations
-Components are designated by letter symbols and numerals (C1-C9)
-After cleavage, in most cases smaller fragment is designated “a” and larger fragment designated “b”
-complexes with enzymatic activity are designated by a line over the complex

C1r is a serine protease that cleaves C1s
Note it takes at least two Fc sites for stable C1-antibody interaction to occur

C4
A glycoprotein with three chains a, b and g
a gets a small piece of the amino terminus shaved off, that exposes a C2 binding site on C4b.
C4b
C2a
Kuby                              Zipfel an Serkel Review 2009

C3b
-Can bind to membrane or immune complexes and promote phagoctyosis
-Can bind to C4b2a to form C4b2a3b or C5 convertase
Converts >200 C3

Mannose (or mannan) Binding Lectin (MBL)
-also binds fucose

Alternative Pathway
b
Microbial surfaces can stabilize C3b produced by slow, spontaneous hydrolysis of C3 to C3b+C3a
Zipfel an Serkel Review 2009

C5b binds to the surface of the target cell and provides a binding site for the subsequent components of the MAC.  C5b is extremely labile and rapidly becomes inactive unless it is stabilized by binding of C6
C5 has two protein chains
C3b
Binding of C7 to C5bC6 exposes hydrophobic regions that bind membrane phospholipids

As many as 10-17 C9 can be bound by a single C5b678 complex. This complex makes a 70-100 angstrom hole
Formation of C5b678 creates a small pore 10 angstrom in diameter
